did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel multi-classification method based on a convolutional neural network (CNN) with a nested residual structure and transfer learning to classify six kinds of skin diseases (nevus, seborrheic keratosis, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, eczema, and basal cell carcinoma) from dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel multi-classification method using a convolutional neural network (CNN) with a nested residual structure. It details the methodology, dataset construction (11,566 dermoscopy images), training via transfer learning, and reports specific performance metrics (65.8% accuracy on six-classification, 90% on two-classification) compared against state-of-the-art networks and dermatologists.

title: Multi-classiﬁcation of skin diseases for dermoscopy images using deep learning
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel multi-classification method using a convolutional neural network (CNN) with a nested residual structure to classify six common skin diseases from dermoscopy images. The dataset comprised 11,566 images collected from Peking Union Medical College Hospital, covering nevus, seborrheic keratosis, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, eczema, and basal cell carcinoma. The network was trained via transfer learning and evaluated on both six-classification and binary classification tasks. On the six-classification task, the method achieved an accuracy of 65.8%, outperforming state-of-the-art networks like ResNet-50 and VGG-16. For the binary classification of nevus versus seborrheic keratosis, the model reached 90% accuracy, significantly exceeding the average performance of 149 professional dermatologists.

all_task_types.target: Six-class classification of skin diseases (nevus, seborrheic keratosis, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, eczema, and basal cell carcinoma) and binary classification (nevus versus seborrheic keratosis).
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a multi-classification method using a CNN with a nested residual structure to classify six kinds of skin diseases from dermoscopy images. It reports an overall accuracy of 65.8% on the six-classification task and 90% on the binary classification task (nevus vs. seborrheic keratosis). The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: Peking Union Medical College Hospital Dermoscopy Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset collected from the Department of Dermatology at Peking Union Medical College Hospital, consisting of dermoscopy images of skin lesions photographed using polarized light dermoscopy with immersion or non-immersion patterns. The study selected a subset of 11,566 images from an initial collection of 28,653 images covering over 200 skin diseases.

use.training: Used for training the proposed CNN with nested residual structure via transfer learning (fine-tuning weights pre-trained on ILSVRC).
use.testing: Used for evaluating the six-classification performance of the proposed method and comparing it against state-of-the-art networks (VGG-16, GoogLeNet, ResNet-50, ResNext-50).
use.other: The dataset was initially collected from 4,748 patients. Malignant melanoma was excluded due to low prevalence (9 cases). Additionally, a separate external set of 60 dermoscopy images (not in the main dataset) was used for binary classification comparison with dermatologists.

datasets_used.name: ILSVRC Dataset
datasets_used.description: The ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge dataset, containing 1.28 million images across 1,000 object categories.

use.training: Used for pre-training the ResNet-50 backbone weights before transfer learning was applied to the dermoscopy dataset.

datasets_used.name: External Dermoscopy Image Set
datasets_used.description: A small set of 60 dermoscopy images used specifically for the binary classification task comparing the model's performance against professional dermatologists. These images were not part of the main 11,566 image dataset.

use.testing: Used for testing the binary classification task (nevus vs. seborrheic keratosis) to compare model accuracy against the average of 149 dermatologists.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize and analyze the classification performance of the proposed CNN and ResNet-50, showing instances of actual vs. predicted classes for the six skin diseases.

To evaluate the performance of the proposed method and comparative networks (VGG-16, GoogLeNet, ResNet-50, ResNext-50) on the six-classification task, and to compare the model's performance against the average accuracy of 149 professional dermatologists on a binary classification task.
To benchmark the deep learning model's performance against human experts (149 dermatologists) on a binary classification task (nevus vs. seborrheic keratosis) using a separate set of 60 images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy metrics (e.g., 65.8% vs 63.6% for six-classification, 90% vs 83.6% for binary classification) without statistical significance testing.

Peking Union Medical College Hospital De.description: Classification of 6 skin diseases (nevus, seborrheic keratosis, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, eczema, basal cell carcinoma) on a test set of 1,150 images.
Peking Union Medical College Hospital De.notes: The proposed method achieved the best overall accuracy among the compared networks. It performed particularly well on eczema (10% higher than ResNet-50). Confusion matrices indicated that nevus and seborrheic keratosis were easily confused, as were psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, and eczema. Basal cell carcinoma was often misdiagnosed as nevus or seborrheic keratosis.

External Dermoscopy Image Set (Binary Cl.description: Binary classification of nevus versus seborrheic keratosis on a separate set of 60 images (30 nevus, 30 seborrheic keratosis) not included in the main dataset.
External Dermoscopy Image Set (Binary Cl.notes: The proposed method significantly outperformed the average performance of 149 professional dermatologists. The method had higher accuracy in both skin diseases compared to the dermatologists.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a nested residual structure
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from weights pre-trained on the ILSVRC dataset (1.28 million images, 1,000 object categories)

architecture_details.modification: Nested residual structure created by adding skip connections to the original residual structure. The network consists of four parts: Part A and Part D contain three regular residual structures each; Part B and Part C (in the middle) combine regular and nested residual structures. The nested residual structure consists of n=2 residual structures and a skip connection linking the input of the first to the output of the last.

training_details.training_schedule: Fine-tuning on the dermoscopy dataset after pre-training on ILSVRC

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with nested residual structure (Our method)
preprocessing_techniques: Normalizing the pixel values of input images to a [0,1] range
conclusions: The proposed CNN with a nested residual structure achieved an overall accuracy of 65.8% on a six-classification task (nevus, seborrheic keratosis, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, eczema, and basal cell carcinoma), outperforming state-of-the-art networks including ResNet-50 (63.6%), VGG-16 (61.8%), GoogLeNet (57.4%), and ResNext-50 (58.0%).

The method achieved an overall accuracy of 90% on a binary classification task (nevus versus seborrheic keratosis), significantly exceeding the average performance of 149 professional dermatologists (83.6%).

The nested residual structure, created by adding skip connections to the original residual structure, improves the network's learning ability by allowing it to learn more information than standard residual structures.
Transfer learning from weights pre-trained on the ILSVRC dataset was effective for training the network on the dermoscopy image dataset.

Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) was difficult to distinguish from nevus and seborrheic keratosis, and psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, and eczema were also difficult to distinguish among each other, indicating challenges in clinical diagnosis for these specific classes.
The proposed method demonstrated better classification ability on each disease class compared to ResNet-50, except for nevus.
important_remarks: The dataset is derived from a single center (Peking Union Medical College Hospital), introducing potential population and geographic bias; malignant melanoma was excluded due to low prevalence (9 cases) in this specific cohort.

The binary classification comparison with dermatologists used a very small external test set of only 60 images (30 per class), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the claim that the model outperforms 149 dermatologists.
No formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported for the comparisons between the deep learning model and state-of-the-art networks or dermatologists.

Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) had a very small sample size (48 cases, 204 images) and achieved the lowest accuracy (38.0%), indicating weak evidence for this specific class and potential data imbalance issues.

The study does not report data leakage risks regarding the relationship between multiple images from the same patient; the dataset contains multiple images per lesion, and it is not explicitly stated if images from the same patient were split across training and test sets.

The model's performance on the six-classification task (65.8% accuracy) is moderate, with significant confusion between clinically similar diseases (e.g., psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, and eczema), highlighting limitations in clinical applicability for these specific differentials.
Transfer learning was used from ILSVRC (natural images) to dermoscopy images, which may introduce domain shift biases not fully addressed in the analysis.
